paragraphs.text: European Tech Alliance Ref. Ares(2025)9112888 - 24/10/2025
OCTOBER 2025

The European Tech Alliance (EUTA) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's consultation on the topic of digital fairness. We fully share the Commission's objective of upholding strong consumer protection in Europe. Consumer trust in digital products and services is a cornerstone of the Single Market, and safeguarding this trust is essential for Europe's competitiveness.

For European tech companies, consumer protection is not an abstract principle but a daily responsibility. EUTA members collectively provide products and services to more than a billion users worldwide, and have invested heavily in compliance with European and national rules, at times going beyond legal requirements to ensure users feel safe, respected, and empowered when engaging online. This commitment is vital: trust in Europe's digital economy begins with robust consumer protection.

section_path: The crucial need to tackle the enforcement gap
paragraphs.text: We believe that enforcement of the comprehensive framework already in place should be the main priority in order to maintain a high level of consumer protection. From the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), e-Privacy Directive and the Digital Services Act (DSA), Digital Market Act (DMA), Platform to Business Regulation to the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Directive (UTCCD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), Accessibility Act, Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) and recent updates through the Enforcement and Modernisation Directive, the European Union operates under some of the world's most extensive consumer protection rules.

section_path: The crucial need to tackle the enforcement gap
paragraphs.text: The pressing challenge is not the absence of legislation, but the uneven application and fragmented enforcement of these obligations across the EU. To deliver fairness, the rules must be applied consistently by all companies operating in Europe, regardless of origin or business model.

section_path: The limitations of the public consultation
paragraphs.text: The structure of the public consultation does not leave sufficient space for the nuances and complexity that the topic of online consumer protection requires. Respondents are often presented with a narrow set of options that cannot fully capture the range of

section_path: The limitations of the public consultation
paragraphs.text: perspectives necessary to address such a multifaceted debate. We are also unable to comment on the policy options presented in cases where we believe no new action is needed. For this reason, we consider it essential to elaborate on our views separately from the questionnaire.

section_path: The limitations of the public consultation
paragraphs.text: In several sections of the consultation, the European Tech Alliance has answered “No actions needed.” This response should not be mistaken for indifference to the challenges consumers face online. The difficulties faced by vulnerable groups, particularly minors, deserve careful attention and effective responses.

Where we diverge is in the policy path being proposed. The Commission’s plan to introduce a new, horizontal framework - layered on top of existing legislation - risks creating more complexity without delivering more protection to European consumers. The claim that an upcoming Digital Fairness Act would merely “fill in the gaps” does not seem to be reflected by the content of the consultation, where many of the options presented are broad, far-reaching, and in some cases duplicative of existing obligations.

If the goal is to improve consumer protection, the right path is clear: boost consumers’ awareness of their existing rights, ensure they are enforced effectively and consistently, and promote a level playing field through legal clarity and simplification. Europe’s competitiveness depends on it.

Europe already has one of the most comprehensive consumer protection frameworks in the world. Adding another horizontal regulation on top of the existing rules is unlikely to make non-compliant actors adhere to them - instead, it risks penalising compliant businesses and further increasing their compliance costs. Since the main challenge so far has been the insufficient application and enforcement of current rules, it is also questionable whether national authorities will have the capacity to effectively enforce this additional layer of regulation.

We provide below feedback from European tech champions on specific policy options in the Public Consultation, and look forward to continuing our constructive dialogue with the Commission going forward.
www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

Dark patterns are not an unregulated phenomenon. The EU has already developed strong and comprehensive frameworks that address manipulative and misleading practices. Different scenarios that could be seen as a "dark pattern" are already covered in this extensive and interlocking regulatory regime. Further legislative action would lead to overprescriptive approaches that could have adverse results for businesses and consumers.

The existing legal framework already addresses manipulative design both substantively and procedurally. The DSA expressly prohibits deceptive or manipulative user interfaces that materially impair users' ability to make free and informed decisions (Article 25) and empowers the Commission to issue guidelines to clarify its application. In parallel, the UCPD captures misleading and aggressive practices (Articles 5-9), including in its list of practices that are banned outright (for instance, falsely claiming that an offer is time-limited). The AI Act also prohibits subliminal techniques, purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques or use of AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities based on age, disability or a specific social or economic situation. Additionally, Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) sweeps in 2022 have also identified problematic patterns and triggered corrective action for companies that were in breach. In short, the toolbox is complete; what is needed is consistent use of the tools already in hand of the enforcement authorities.

section_path: Enforcement is needed, not duplication of rules
paragraphs.text: The real challenge lies in the inconsistent enforcement of existing legislation across the EU. Currently, we see varying and uncoordinated enforcement between authorities looking at the same topic from different perspectives (e.g. personalisation looked at by consumer, privacy and digital authorities) in one Member State, but also between those authorities across the EU. To address this, stronger and more coordinated enforcement is essential. In particular, a revision and strengthening of the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation could ensure that consumer protection rules are applied consistently, effectively, and across borders.

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Taking a blanket approach over which practices are "dark patterns" risks undermining businesses' legitimate interest to market their products and services through reasonable marketing practices, including articulating the benefits of a contract. It also risks depriving the consumer of important information and interfering with a smooth online experience: design choices such as the number of clicks before a consumer decision, legitimate urgency or scarcity messages, can provide important information to the consumer before making a choice or are crucial to build a smooth experience providing the relevant information to enable an informed choice at the right moment.

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Additionally, some of the policy ideas presented in the consultation are problematic. The idea of regulating companies' freedom to "present choices in a leading manner" overlooks

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: that such design features often help guide consumers toward safer, more sustainable, or more beneficial outcomes. Requirements concerning which colour businesses can use in their interfaces would stifle product and marketing to innovation and would contradict the general ethos of principles-based regulation. For example, some companies might stand out using a recognisable colour (be it green, orange or red), making that interface unique and defined from competitors. Maintaining a consistent approach between the online and offline environments is equally important: offline retailers are fully entitled to "present choices in a leading manner," "use colours," or "display stock availability", and the same should apply to the online world.

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Furthermore, the inclusion of elements such as "click fatigue" in the consumer law debate is misplaced. This is primarily a matter for ePrivacy directive, not consumer protection law, and should not be addressed under the upcoming Digital Fairness Act. Expanding the scope of consumer law to cover such topics risks blurring the boundaries between legal frameworks and undermining coherence.

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: To tackle the issue of dark patterns, the EUTA considers the following to be the most effective ways forward:

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Modernise and update existing guidelines without introducing new obligations

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Encourage and facilitate consistent interpretation across Member States

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: Strengthen cooperation among regulators to close enforcement gaps

section_path: The policy options presented are inherently worrying
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

EUTA shares the Commission's view that protecting minors from addictive designs online is a top priority. Those of our members providing services to users aged under 18 have made considerable investments in age-appropriate online experiences and additional safeguards where necessary.

Operating a competitive online business relies on offering consumers an easy and smooth user experience. This is fundamentally different from encouraging digital addiction in ways that cause harm. We urge the Commission to clearly distinguish design features that are proven to be addictive and harmful from legitimate product features that promote engagement with digital services. It is essential to avoid overly wide or vague definitions of "addictive features" and to be aware of unintended consequences of applying broad requirements in this area.

Practices around online design are already regulated through various pieces of legislation. For example, online platforms are already subject to the obligations stemming from DSA's article 25, which forbids the use of dark patterns and by extension the use of addictive designs. Addictive design is also addressed via the AI Act (Article 5). As these rules have only recently come into effect, their impacts were not assessed in the Digital Fitness Check that underpin this public consultation. It is therefore crucial to focus on the implementation of existing rules on online platforms, before expanding these obligations more widely.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: The policy options outlined in the European Commission's public consultation emphasize the need for stronger control over so-called "addictive design features," with the suggestion that such features might need to be "switched off by default." However, the lack of a clear and precise definition of what constitutes an "addictive design" is problematic. Without a precise definition that is supported by evidence of harm, policymakers risk blurring the line between manipulative designs aimed at exploiting vulnerabilities and neutral or beneficial design features that are widely accepted by users.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: Autoplay functionalities provide a concrete illustration of this issue. According to the policy options listed, autoplay could be understood as "any video or audio file that plays without user intervention." Such a blanket interpretation would go well beyond harmful use cases and would also encompass widely used and legitimate features, such as the continuous playback of music playlists. These functions are not inherently addictive - rather, they contribute to a smooth and enjoyable user experience that many consumers actively expect and value. Treating them as problematic by default would distort the regulatory intent and diminish consumer choice.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: Similarly, we are concerned at the broad reference to "recommender systems that are steered to increase consumer engagement". Recommender systems are a core element of

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: the modern digital experience, helping users discover relevant content, products, or services in a vast information environment. While it is important to identify and prevent manipulative practices that deliberately exploit vulnerabilities, especially in the case of minors, the majority of recommender system use cases online are neither addictive nor harmful. Overregulation in this area could inadvertently penalise legitimate practices, such as tailored product, book, music, film, food, or educational content recommendations, and create friction in the consumer journey that companies work hard to optimise. It's worth noting that recommender systems are a significant driver of discovery of creative content and cultural diversity - a stated goal of this Commission.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: In this context, a more proportionate and effective approach would be to target specific harmful practices rather than introducing sweeping prohibitions or limits on broadly defined product features. Principles are more effective than checklists, because they can be applied in different scenarios and because checklists typically fail to keep pace with market, technological and behavioral developments. Focusing on intent and actual or likely harm prevents inadvertently outlawing benign patterns that improve clarity or safety - such as prompts that help users avoid fraud or flows that verify understanding of a contractual commitment.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: Additionally, adding a new layer of rules risks ossifying design, with a risk of clashing with accessibility standards (including WCAG and EN 301 549), and diverting resources from innovative European companies from improvements in safety, accessibility and consumer trust. Therefore, having new rules layered atop the existing rulebook would produce cost without added consumer protection.

section_path: The policy options presented hold strong risks of unintended consequences
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Specific features in digital products such as video games
paragraphs.text: While these issues are important in their own right, they fall largely outside the operational scope of EUTA members' business activities.

section_path: Specific features in digital products such as video games
paragraphs.text: That said, we strongly emphasize the importance of effective enforcement of the existing consumer protection framework that already addresses these types of practices. The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), in particular, provides a robust legal basis for tackling manipulative or misleading design strategies in digital environments, including those employed in gaming. The priority for the European Commission should therefore not be the introduction of additional regulatory instruments, but rather the consistent and uniform application of the rules already in place across Member States.

section_path: Specific features in digital products such as video games
paragraphs.text: Recent developments underscore this need. Some e-commerce platforms, notably Temu and Shein, have introduced gamification elements onto their platforms such as penalties against disengagement. These practices can and should be scrutinized under the existing provisions of the UCPD.

section_path: Specific features in digital products such as video games
paragraphs.text: For this reason, we believe that the European Commission's main focus should be on strengthening the enforcement of current rules rather than drafting new, overlapping legislation. Ensuring that authorities have the tools, resources, and cross-border cooperation mechanisms necessary to enforce the UCPD effectively will deliver greater consumer protection, provide more legal certainty for businesses, and prevent regulatory inflation.

section_path: Specific features in digital products such as video games
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Personalisation is a cornerstone of the digital economy
paragraphs.text: Personalisation is a cornerstone of the modern digital economy. It allows consumers to benefit from products and services that are more relevant, user-friendly, and tailored to their needs – from personalised music playlists to customised shopping recommendations. When implemented responsibly, personalisation enhances the consumer experience and discovery, reduces information overload, and strengthens trust in digital services. For European tech businesses, it enables more efficient communication with their customers and is in many ways central to competitiveness and consumer trust.

section_path: Personalisation is a cornerstone of the digital economy
paragraphs.text: We share the concern that the systematic exploitation of consumers' vulnerabilities to personalise commercial offers is unacceptable. However, this type of practice is already comprehensively regulated under existing legislation. Adding a new horizontal layer of rules through the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA) risks creating unnecessary duplication and conflicts with existing frameworks, thereby undermining the proper functioning of the single market.

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: Unfair commercial practices, including exploitative or manipulative advertising, are already regulated under current EU law. In particular, the GDPR and ePrivacy Directive provide strong safeguards for consumers in relation to personal data processing.

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: The GDPR applies horizontally and covers all consumers, including vulnerable individuals, through provisions on transparency, choice, rights of access, deletion, rectification, portability, and the right to lodge complaints before Data Protection Authorities (DPAs). It also places strict limits on the processing of sensitive personal data, broadly interpreted by courts to include health-related information and data inferred indirectly (e.g. information about mental health, addictions, or financial hardship). Processing such data typically requires explicit consent, is considered high-risk, and triggers the obligation to conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA).

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: More broadly, the GDPR's rules on accountability, legal bases, risk assessment, profiling, or automated decision making also ensure that data processing activities based on personalization, including advertising, do not lead to unfair outcomes. This relies on the fairness principle of Article 5(1)(a) which is one of the umbrella core principles of the GDPR. This principle requires considering how the data processing affects the interests of the people concerned, as a group and individually to capture potential unfair practices that go beyond the processing of sensitive personal data (such as financial hardship, socio-economic status...). This means also that the data protection by design and by default principles or Article 25 already encompasses a "fairness by design" principle in itself. Adding a "fairness by design" in the DFA would only serve to duplicate the existing rules of the GDPR.

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: Consequently, because of its wide, open and future-proof nature, the GDPR already addresses the practices that the DFA would seek to address under the exploitation of consumers' vulnerabilities to personalised commercial offers. Additional guidance by the

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: EDPB (in coordination with relevant consumer authorities if needed) could clarify how the GDPR applies to the processing of data for vulnerable individuals, taking into consideration the existing case law.

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act (DSA) complements these protections by introducing obligations on transparency in advertising (Article 26) and banning profiling based on sensitive data, as well as advertising to minors. These obligations apply not only to platforms but also to actors throughout the advertising supply chain who work with these online platforms.

section_path: Existing rules already cover unfair personalisation practices
paragraphs.text: Taken together, these frameworks already provide robust safeguards against unfair personalisation. In cases where further clarification is needed, particularly concerning vulnerable individuals, additional guidance from the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), in coordination with consumer authorities, would be more appropriate than new legislation.

section_path: Risks of duplication and legal conflicts
paragraphs.text: Introducing a new set of rules under the DFA to regulate unfair personalisation would create overlaps with the GDPR, ePrivacy Directive, DSA, and UCPD. This would complicate compliance by subjecting businesses to multiple overlapping obligations enforced by different regulators, creating legal uncertainty.

section_path: Risks of duplication and legal conflicts
paragraphs.text: It also risks undermining data minimisation under the GDPR. For companies to identify "vulnerable consumers" as required by a possible DFA obligation, they would likely need to collect more personal data, potentially sensitive data, which may not be lawful without explicit consent. This would put companies in a regulatory dilemma: either collect more intrusive data (increasing GDPR compliance risks) or risk unknowingly targeting a vulnerable person and breaching the DFA.

section_path: Risks of duplication and legal conflicts
paragraphs.text: In addition, the notion of vulnerable consumers is already covered in key legislations (GDPR, DSA, Accessibility Act), providing legal stability for companies. Broadening the scope, particularly with terms such as "emotional distress" or "negative mental state" risks diluting its impact and clarity, as well as providing subjective definitions.

section_path: Risks of duplication and legal conflicts
paragraphs.text: Therefore, the EU should focus on specific, well-defined at-risk groups, such as minors that ensure effective protection without introducing disproportionate obligations on businesses.

section_path: Risks of duplication and legal conflicts
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: The conversation around harmful practices by social media influencers raises legitimate questions, particularly with respect to transparency, consumer protection, and the specific influence influencers can have on minors. However, when considering the appropriate policy response, structural challenges emerge.

section_path: Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: There is a lack of clear and harmonised definitions at EU level. The concept of "influencer" itself is not uniformly defined across Member States, which leads to considerable ambiguity in enforcement and compliance. Without a clear legal definition, new regulatory obligations risk capturing a wide variety of online actors while leaving enforcement gaps unaddressed.

section_path: Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: In addition, there is a clear lack of enforcement of existing rules. The Digital Services Act (DSA) and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) already provide a strong basis to address harmful or manipulative practices in influencer content. However, enforcement has so far been uneven, with Digital Services Coordinators (DSCs), national audiovisual regulators as well as other national authorities not yet fully equipped or coordinated to apply these rules effectively.

section_path: Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: For these reasons, EUTA does not consider the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to be the appropriate instrument to regulate influencer practices. Adding horizontal obligations under the DFA would not resolve the underlying issues of definitional clarity or enforcement capacity. Instead, it risks duplicating existing frameworks and further complicating the regulatory landscape.

section_path: Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Enforcement of existing rules should be a priority
paragraphs.text: EUTA considers that challenges relating to “unfair marketing related to pricing” should be addressed within the framework of existing legislation, such as the CRD, the UCPD and in particular the Price Indication Directive (PID). The issues identified in this area are not the result of a lack of legislation, but rather of fragmented implementation and uneven enforcement across Member States.

section_path: Enforcement of existing rules should be a priority
paragraphs.text: Rather than introducing a new, horizontal layer of regulation through the proposed DFA, which risks duplicating or even conflicting with established legal frameworks, the European Commission should prioritize harmonised enforcement, consistent interpretation, and targeted guidance on the application of the PID, CRD and UCPD. This approach would reduce legal uncertainty and compliance costs for businesses while ensuring that consumers benefit from transparent and fair practices across the Single Market.

section_path: Enforcement of existing rules should be a priority
paragraphs.text: In particular, new guidelines could usefully clarify the formal requirements for announcing price reductions and for presenting price comparisons, so that consumers can better understand and evaluate the offers presented to them. However, these guidelines should be developed in close consultation with the business community, to ensure they are practical, avoid unintended standardisation of interfaces, and respect sector-specific requirements and the freedom of commercial communication. This targeted approach would reduce legal uncertainty while ensuring that consumers across the Single Market benefit from fair and transparent pricing practices.

section_path: Enforcement of existing rules should be a priority
paragraphs.text: In this context, EUTA’s position in this consultation is clear: no new legislative actions such as the DFA are needed. However, efforts should focus on guidance to clarify how the PID applies in a digital environment, coordinated enforcement to ensure more consistent application across Member States, and possibly a simplification effort through an omnibus approach.

EUTA is particularly concerned about the possible unintended consequences of introducing regulatory measures that directly target pricing mechanisms. While the ambition to address harmful practices is legitimate, a blanket ban on certain pricing practices risks capturing legitimate, consumer-beneficial business models.

For example, dynamic pricing can be used for dynamic “pricing down” strategies to reduce waste (e.g. in perishable goods sectors), optimise capacity (e.g. in hospitality sector), or to provide consumers with affordable access. In some cases, prices are set by the suppliers, not by the platform. Overly prescriptive regulation restricting all types of dynamic pricing could unintentionally prohibit such beneficial practices, undermining both business sustainability and consumer welfare.
Likewise, drip pricing should be carefully defined and a categorical ban on the presentation of any mandatory costs/fees during the ordering process should be avoided.
www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

Such a ban would be ignoring the cases where fees are shown progressively based on the user's choices as they build their basket or due to operational necessities (e.g. where customized costs can only be calculated when all necessary user inputs have been provided). So long as consumers are informed about the costs in a clear, consistent and transparent manner up until the final step before their order is confirmed, such practices are not aimed at misleading.

Moreover, regulating pricing in a broad manner could distort competition by limiting the ability of companies to adapt to market demand, reduce consumer choice, as innovative models offering discounts or tailored pricing would become restricted, and create compliance complexity, especially for SMEs, who may lack the resources to navigate additional layers of regulation. It is also crucial to keep in mind that competition on price is a legitimate and desirable form of competition that leads to price benefits for consumers.

EUTA therefore urges caution in considering new interventions in this space. Targeted enforcement against genuinely misleading or exploitative practices through existing rules like the UCPD or PID remains the most effective and proportionate approach. A horizontal regulation of pricing under the DFA risks creating unnecessary constraints and undermining well-functioning market practices that ultimately benefit consumers.
www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

EUTA fully agrees that consumers should know what they are signing up for, and have easy channels to cancel their digital contracts at any time. However, we stress that the existing legal framework, most notably the revised Consumer Rights Directive (CRD) and the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), already provides a robust basis for ensuring transparency, fairness, and effective consumer rights.

Instead of introducing new, horizontal obligations via the Digital Fairness Act, the European Commission should prioritise consistent enforcement, practical guidance, and simplification of the current legislative framework.

A stronger focus on enforcement, coupled with targeted guidance, would also support the EU's broader simplification agenda, ensuring that compliant businesses are not penalised by the layering of new and duplicative rules. At the same time, consumers would benefit from greater clarity and more consistent application of rights they already hold.

section_path: Reminders for renewals and free trials
paragraphs.text: EUTA stresses that existing consumer protection legislation already requires businesses to provide clear pre-contractual information regarding renewals and trial-to-paid conversions. Imposing additional requirements, such as frequent reminder notices, risks being disproportionate and counterproductive:

section_path: Reminders for renewals and free trials
paragraphs.text: For short-term contracts that renew weekly or monthly, additional reminders would lead to information overload for consumers, undermining the consumer experience and risking that they ignore or miss genuinely important communications, for example regarding their price plan or account security. Repetitive “look-a-like” communications also directly increase consumers’ vulnerability to phishing scams.

section_path: Reminders for renewals and free trials
paragraphs.text: EUTA does not believe auto-renewal reminders for contracts with regular billing cycles are justified provided consumers are able to cancel at any time, without penalty. In the case of physical goods subscriptions, reminder notices are equally unnecessary, as the regular delivery of physical goods serves as a natural reminder and makes it highly unlikely that a customer will forget about them.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: While consumer awareness is important, EUTA believes that introducing an opt-in requirement for renewals or trial-to-paid conversions would not improve consumer protection and could create unintended negative consequences.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: Consumers are already informed of contract terms through pre-contractual information obligations. Ensuring strict enforcement of these existing requirements should provide the necessary safeguards. An additional opt-in mechanism carries a high risk of being missed by consumers, which could result in unintended contract terminations, loss of account data, or missed deliveries.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: For contracts that renew automatically after a free trial, an opt-in is unnecessary if consumers have been clearly informed in advance of the conversion terms. For ongoing

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: paid subscriptions, opt-ins are even less justified, since consumers are already actively paying and aware of their obligations.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: As with reminders, short-term subscriptions and physical goods subscriptions should be excluded from any new obligations. Their built-in structures provide sufficient consumer awareness without imposing redundant requirements.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: EUTA agrees that contracts concluded online should be easy and straightforward to cancel. This principle is already well-established in EU law.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: We are therefore concerned that the upcoming Digital Fairness Act will lead to prescriptive product or user experience requirements regarding how cancellation flows should look and feel. Europe's digital economy is incredibly diverse and dynamic, and businesses must retain flexibility to process and execute cancellation requests in ways that make sense for their consumers.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: The Consumer Rights Directive (CRD) grants a 14-day withdrawal right for distance contracts and requires clear, prominent pre-contract information, while the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) addresses so-called "subscription traps" - including, in Article 9(d), unlawful onerous or disproportionate non-contractual barriers when a consumer wishes to exercise contractual rights. Obstructing the cancellation process is also considered a dark pattern, which is banned for online platforms under the DSA (Article 25). These instruments give enforcers the levers they need to correct poor withdrawal designs and deter abusive practices.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: It is also important to preserve the balance between consumer and trader interests. Signing up for a subscription online typically requires multiple steps, including account creation, payment details, and offer selection. The cancellation process should be straightforward, but it should also allow businesses to freely communicate with consumers, for example about what will happen once they cancel their contract. Businesses should also retain the freedom to request feedback to improve services, alert customers to outstanding deliveries or obligations to ensure clarity, and make retention offers, provided these do not unreasonably delay or complicate the process.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: At the same time, blanket refund entitlements or across-the-board cancellation prescriptions would risk fraud and undermine legitimate recurring models that enable low prices and predictable service for consumers. Therefore, any policy adjustment should remain coherent with consumer-payments rules and contract-law principles to avoid unintended conflicts and legal uncertainty.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: All in all, the proportionate course is guidance and consistent application of existing rules, leaving space for accessible, technology-neutral solutions, rather than imposing fixed layouts that reduce usability without improving protection. Furthermore, before considering any new measures, it would be prudent to allow the Directive (EU) 2023/2673 as regards financial services contracts concluded at a distance, which introduces the withdrawal button, to be fully transposed and implemented across Member States. This will provide the necessary time for the Directive to take effect and demonstrate its impact on consumer protection.

section_path: Opt-in requirements for renewals and trial conversions
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

While simplification measures in the field of consumer protection are both necessary and welcome, the current proposal to introduce a new horizontal layer of obligations through a Digital Fairness Act (DFA) risks undermining this very objective. Instead of reducing complexity, this approach would impose additional compliance requirements on European tech companies, many of which are already navigating a dense and fragmented regulatory environment.

Adding a substantial number of burdensome obligations on businesses, while only marginally streamlining certain existing measures, cannot reasonably be described as simplification. On the contrary, the result would be an overall increase in regulatory fragmentation, legal uncertainty, and compliance costs, particularly for companies that lack the resources to adapt quickly to overlapping rules.

The issues the DFA intends to address have already been, to a large extent, considered in existing EU legislation. Recent regulatory frameworks, including the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), and sector-specific consumer protection rules, already address many of the practices the DFA aims to cover. Where gaps have been identified, such as through the findings of the European Commission's Digital Fairness Fitness Check, these should be addressed through targeted updates of the relevant, dedicated pieces of legislation. This approach would strengthen coherence and avoid unnecessary duplication.

Ultimately, the current focus of EU consumer policy should not be the introduction of yet another broad legislative instrument, but rather the steady and consistent enforcement of existing rules and digital literacy for consumers to strengthen informed choice. Enhancing the enforcement framework, for example through a reform of the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation, would ensure more uniform application across Member States and provide consumers with the legal certainty they deserve, while minimizing unnecessary regulatory burdens on businesses.

section_path: Redefining established concepts and introducing vague new ones
paragraphs.text: We encourage the European Commission to take a careful and proportionate approach when revisiting core consumer protection concepts such as "vulnerabilities" and the "reasonable consumer". These notions are long-standing and well-tested in EU law, providing predictability for businesses and strong safeguards for consumers. Introducing new and untested concepts - such as "fairness by design" - risks creating legal uncertainty, economic disadvantages and compliance challenges without clear evidence of added benefit.

section_path: Redefining established concepts and introducing vague new ones
paragraphs.text: In particular, the idea of "digital vulnerabilities" raises significant practical difficulties for online intermediaries such as second-hand marketplaces. Our role is largely limited to connecting consumers who wish to exchange or sell goods, and we have little visibility or control over the transaction itself. Expecting platforms to detect or mitigate temporary or highly subjective vulnerabilities would be disproportionate and technically unfeasible, requiring data collection beyond what is permitted under the GDPR. A risk-based approach, consistent with the DSA and the GDPR, would better account for the different levels of responsibility and risk among digital actors.

section_path: Redefining established concepts and introducing vague new ones
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

section_path: Redefining established concepts and introducing vague new ones
paragraphs.text: Similarly, the proposal to embed “fairness by design” across all services and traders—regardless of their size, audience, or risk profile — appears too broad and conceptually uncertain. Fairness is an evolving and context-dependent notion, difficult to operationalise in technical terms. A uniform obligation could stifle innovation and impose unnecessary burdens, especially on second-hand marketplaces with distinct characteristics and a lower consumer risk profile. Preserving established concepts such as the “reasonable consumer” standard ensures both effective consumer protection and the legal certainty companies need to innovate responsibly.

section_path: Redefining established concepts and introducing vague new ones
paragraphs.text: www.eutechalliance.eu | info@eutechalliance.eu

EUTA represents leading European tech companies that provide innovative products and services to over a billion users globally. Our 36 EUTA member companies from 15 European countries are popular and have earned the trust of consumers. As companies born and bred in Europe, for whom the EU is a crucial market, we have a deep commitment to European citizens and values.

With the right conditions, our companies can strengthen Europe's resilience and technological autonomy, protect and empower users online, and promote Europe's values of transparency, rule of law and innovation to the rest of the world. The EUTA calls for boosting Europe's tech competitiveness by having an ambitious EU tech strategy to overcome growth obstacles, making a political commitment to clear, targeted and risk-based rules, and enforcing rules consistently to match the globalised market we are in.
Visit us at www.eutechalliance.eu. The European Tech Alliance, Square de Meeûs 35, 1000 Brussels, Belgium
